what: Leveraging Experiment Lines to Data Analytics
title: Leveraging Experiment Lines to Data Analytics
text: goal DAL Toolbox provide series data analytics functions organized framework. supports data preprocessing, classification, regression, clustering, time series prediction functions.

title: Leveraging Experiment Lines to Data Analytics
text: can install development version dal GitHub :

title: Leveraging Experiment Lines to Data Analytics
text: DAL Toolbox examples made available : https://nbviewer.org/github/cefet-rj-dal/daltoolbox-examples/tree/main/ demo video provided : https://eic.cefet-rj.br/~dal/leveraging-experiment-lines--data-analytics/ examples organized according general (data preprocessing), clustering, classification, regression, time series functions.
code: library(daltoolbox) #> Registered S3 method overwritten by 'quantmod': #>   method            from #>   as.zoo.data.frame zoo #>  #> Attaching package: 'daltoolbox' #> The following object is masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     transform ## loading DAL Toolbox

title: Boston Housing Data (Regression) — Boston
text: housing values suburbs Boston. crim: per capita crime rate town. zn: proportion residential land zoned lots 25,000 sq.ft. indus: proportion non-retail business acres per town chas: Charles River dummy variable (= 1 tract bounds) nox: nitric oxides concentration (parts per 10 million) rm: average number rooms per dwelling age: proportion owner-occupied units built prior 1940 dis: weighted distances five Boston employment centres rad: index accessibility radial highways tax: full-value property-tax rate per $10,000 ptratio: pupil-teacher ratio town black: 1000(Bk - 0.63)^2 Bk proportion blacks town lstat: percentage lower status population medv: Median value owner-occupied homes $1000's

title: Boston Housing Data (Regression) — Boston
text: Creator: Harrison, D. Rubinfeld, D.L. Hedonic prices demand clean air, J. Environ. Economics & Management, vol.5, 81-102, 1978.

title: Action implementation for transform — action.dal_transform
text: default function defines action proxy transform method

title: Action — action
text: Executes action model applied provided data

title: Action — action
text: obj object: dal_base object apply transformation input dataset. ... optional arguments.

title: Action — action
text: result action model applied provided data

title: Action — action
code: data(iris) # an example is minmax normalization trans <- minmax() trans <- fit(trans, iris) tiris <- action(trans, iris)

title: adjust categorical mapping — adjust_class_label
text: x vector categorized valTrue value represent true valFalse value represent false

title: adjust factors — adjust_factor
text: value vector converted factor ilevels order categorical values slevels labels categorical values

title: Categorical mapping — categ_mapping
text: Categorical mapping provides way map levels categorical variable new values. possible value converted binary attribute.

title: Categorical mapping — categ_mapping
text: data frame binary attributes, one possible category.

title: Categorical mapping — categ_mapping
code: cm <- categ_mapping("Species") iris_cm <- transform(cm, iris)  # can be made in a single column species <- iris[,"Species", drop=FALSE] iris_cm <- transform(cm, species)

title: Decision Tree for classification — cla_dtree
text: Creates classification object uses Decision Tree algorithm classification. wraps tree library.

title: Decision Tree for classification — cla_dtree
text: attribute attribute target model building. slevels possible values target classification.

title: Decision Tree for classification — cla_dtree
text: classification object uses Decision Tree algorithm classification.

title: Decision Tree for classification — cla_dtree
code: data(iris) slevels <- levels(iris$Species) model <- cla_dtree("Species", slevels)  # preparing dataset for random sampling sr <- sample_random() sr <- train_test(sr, iris) train <- sr$train test <- sr$test  model <- fit(model, train)  prediction <- predict(model, test) predictand <- adjust_class_label(test[,"Species"]) test_eval <- evaluate(model, predictand, prediction) test_eval$metrics #>    accuracy        f1 sensitivity specificity precision    recall #> 1 0.8888889 0.8333333   0.8333333   0.9166667 0.8333333 0.8333333

title: K Nearest Neighbor Classification — cla_knn
text: Classifies using K-Nearest Neighbor algorithm. wraps class library.

title: K Nearest Neighbor Classification — cla_knn
text: attribute attribute target model building. slevels Possible values target classification. k vector integers indicating number neighbors considered.

title: K Nearest Neighbor Classification — cla_knn
code: data(iris) slevels <- levels(iris$Species) model <- cla_knn("Species", slevels, k=3)  # preparing dataset for random sampling sr <- sample_random() sr <- train_test(sr, iris) train <- sr$train test <- sr$test  model <- fit(model, train)  prediction <- predict(model, test) predictand <- adjust_class_label(test[,"Species"]) test_eval <- evaluate(model, predictand, prediction) test_eval$metrics #>   accuracy f1 sensitivity specificity precision recall #> 1        1  1           1           1         1      1

title: Majority Classification — cla_majority
text: function creates classification object uses majority vote strategy predict target attribute. Given target attribute, function counts number occurrences value dataset selects one appears often.

title: Majority Classification — cla_majority
text: attribute attribute target model building. slevels Possible values target classification.

title: Majority Classification — cla_majority
code: data(iris) slevels <- levels(iris$Species) model <- cla_majority("Species", slevels)  # preparing dataset for random sampling sr <- sample_random() sr <- train_test(sr, iris) train <- sr$train test <- sr$test  model <- fit(model, train)  prediction <- predict(model, test) predictand <- adjust_class_label(test[,"Species"]) test_eval <- evaluate(model, predictand, prediction) test_eval$metrics #>    accuracy  f1 sensitivity specificity precision recall #> 1 0.4666667 0.2         0.2         0.6       0.2    0.2

title: MLP for classification — cla_mlp
text: Creates classification object uses Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) method. wraps nnet library.

title: MLP for classification — cla_mlp
code: cla_mlp(attribute, slevels, size = NULL, decay = 0.1, maxit = 1000)

title: MLP for classification — cla_mlp
text: attribute attribute target model building slevels possible values target classification size number nodes used hidden layer decay quickly decreases gradient descent maxit maximum iterations

title: MLP for classification — cla_mlp
code: data(iris) slevels <- levels(iris$Species) model <- cla_mlp("Species", slevels, size=3, decay=0.03)  # preparing dataset for random sampling sr <- sample_random() sr <- train_test(sr, iris) train <- sr$train test <- sr$test  model <- fit(model, train)  prediction <- predict(model, test) predictand <- adjust_class_label(test[,"Species"]) test_eval <- evaluate(model, predictand, prediction) test_eval$metrics #>   accuracy f1 sensitivity specificity precision recall #> 1        1  1           1           1         1      1

title: Naive Bayes Classifier — cla_nb
text: Classification using Naive Bayes algorithm wraps e1071 library.

title: Naive Bayes Classifier — cla_nb
text: attribute attribute target model building. slevels Possible values target classification.

title: Naive Bayes Classifier — cla_nb
code: data(iris) slevels <- levels(iris$Species) model <- cla_nb("Species", slevels)  # preparing dataset for random sampling sr <- sample_random() sr <- train_test(sr, iris) train <- sr$train test <- sr$test  model <- fit(model, train)  prediction <- predict(model, test) predictand <- adjust_class_label(test[,"Species"]) test_eval <- evaluate(model, predictand, prediction) test_eval$metrics #>   accuracy f1 sensitivity specificity precision recall #> 1        1  1           1           1         1      1

title: Random Forest for classification — cla_rf
text: Creates classification object uses Random Forest method wraps randomForest library.

title: Random Forest for classification — cla_rf
code: cla_rf(attribute, slevels, nodesize = 5, ntree = 10, mtry = NULL)

title: Random Forest for classification — cla_rf
text: attribute attribute target model building slevels possible values target classification nodesize node size ntree number trees mtry number attributes build tree

title: Random Forest for classification — cla_rf
code: data(iris) slevels <- levels(iris$Species) model <- cla_rf("Species", slevels, ntree=5)  # preparing dataset for random sampling sr <- sample_random() sr <- train_test(sr, iris) train <- sr$train test <- sr$test  model <- fit(model, train)  prediction <- predict(model, test) predictand <- adjust_class_label(test[,"Species"]) test_eval <- evaluate(model, predictand, prediction) test_eval$metrics #>    accuracy        f1 sensitivity specificity precision    recall #> 1 0.9777778 0.9666667   0.9666667   0.9833333 0.9666667 0.9666667

title: SVM for classification — cla_svm
text: Creates classification object uses Support Vector Machine (SVM) method classification wraps e1071 library.

title: SVM for classification — cla_svm
text: attribute attribute target model building slevels possible values target classification epsilon parameter controls width margin around separating hyperplane cost parameter controls trade-wide margin correctly classifying training data points kernel type kernel function used SVM algorithm (linear, radial, polynomial, sigmoid)

title: SVM for classification — cla_svm
code: data(iris) slevels <- levels(iris$Species) model <- cla_svm("Species", slevels, epsilon=0.0,cost=20.000)  # preparing dataset for random sampling sr <- sample_random() sr <- train_test(sr, iris) train <- sr$train test <- sr$test  model <- fit(model, train)  prediction <- predict(model, test) predictand <- adjust_class_label(test[,"Species"]) test_eval <- evaluate(model, predictand, prediction) test_eval$metrics #>    accuracy        f1 sensitivity specificity precision    recall #> 1 0.9777778 0.9666667   0.9666667   0.9833333 0.9666667 0.9666667

title: Classification Tune — cla_tune
text: base_model base model tuning folds number folds cross-validation metric metric used optimize

title: Classification Tune — cla_tune
code: # preparing dataset for random sampling sr <- sample_random() sr <- train_test(sr, iris) train <- sr$train test <- sr$test  # hyper parameter setup tune <- cla_tune(cla_svm("Species", levels(iris$Species))) ranges <- list(epsilon=seq(0,1,0.25), cost=seq(25,100,25), kernel = c("radial"))  # hyper parameter optimization model <- fit(tune, train, ranges)  # testing optimization test_prediction <- predict(model, test) test_predictand <- adjust_class_label(test[,"Species"]) test_eval <- evaluate(model, test_predictand, test_prediction) test_eval$metrics #>    accuracy        f1 sensitivity specificity precision    recall #> 1 0.9555556 0.9333333   0.9333333   0.9666667 0.9333333 0.9333333

text: attribute attribute target model building slevels possible values target classification

title: Clustering Tune — clu_tune
code: data(iris)  # fit model model <- clu_tune(cluster_kmeans(k = 0)) ranges <- list(k = 1:10) model <- fit(model, iris[,1:4], ranges) model$k #> [1] 10

title: DBSCAN — cluster_dbscan
text: Creates clusterer object uses DBSCAN method wraps dbscan library.

title: DBSCAN — cluster_dbscan
text: minPts minimum number points eps distance value

title: DBSCAN — cluster_dbscan
code: # setup clustering model <- cluster_dbscan(minPts = 3)  #load dataset data(iris)  # build model model <- fit(model, iris[,1:4]) clu <- cluster(model, iris[,1:4]) table(clu) #> clu #>  0  1  2  3  4  #> 26 47 38  4 35   # evaluate model using external metric eval <- evaluate(model, clu, iris$Species) eval #> $clusters_entropy #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>   x        ce   qtd    ceg #>   <fct> <dbl> <int>  <dbl> #> 1 0     1.18     26 0.205  #> 2 1     0        47 0      #> 3 2     0        38 0      #> 4 3     0         4 0      #> 5 4     0.422    35 0.0985 #>  #> $clustering_entropy #> [1] 0.3037218 #>  #> $data_entropy #> [1] 1.584963 #>

title: k-means — cluster_kmeans
text: Creates clusterer object uses k-means method wraps stats library.

title: k-means — cluster_kmeans
code: # setup clustering model <- cluster_kmeans(k=3)  #load dataset data(iris)  # build model model <- fit(model, iris[,1:4]) clu <- cluster(model, iris[,1:4]) table(clu) #> clu #>  1  2  3  #> 62 50 38   # evaluate model using external metric eval <- evaluate(model, clu, iris$Species) eval #> $clusters_entropy #> # A tibble: 3 × 4 #>   x        ce   qtd    ceg #>   <fct> <dbl> <int>  <dbl> #> 1 1     0.771    62 0.319  #> 2 2     0        50 0      #> 3 3     0.297    38 0.0754 #>  #> $clustering_entropy #> [1] 0.3938863 #>  #> $data_entropy #> [1] 1.584963 #>

title: PAM — cluster_pam
code: # setup clustering model <- cluster_pam(k = 3)  #load dataset data(iris)  # build model model <- fit(model, iris[,1:4]) clu <- cluster(model, iris[,1:4]) table(clu) #> clu #>  1  2  3  #> 50 62 38   # evaluate model using external metric eval <- evaluate(model, clu, iris$Species) eval #> $clusters_entropy #> # A tibble: 3 × 4 #>   x        ce   qtd    ceg #>   <fct> <dbl> <int>  <dbl> #> 1 1     0        50 0      #> 2 2     0.771    62 0.319  #> 3 3     0.297    38 0.0754 #>  #> $clustering_entropy #> [1] 0.3938863 #>  #> $data_entropy #> [1] 1.584963 #>

title: Data Sample — data_sample
text: data_sample function R used randomly sample data given data frame. can used obtain subset data analysis modeling. Two basic specializations data_sample sample_random sample_stratified. provide random sampling stratified sampling, respectively. Data sample provides training testing partitioning (train_test) k-fold partitioning (k_fold) data.

title: Data Sample — data_sample
code: #using random sampling sample <- sample_random() tt <- train_test(sample, iris)  # distribution of train table(tt$train$Species) #>  #>     setosa versicolor  virginica  #>         40         36         44   # preparing dataset into four folds folds <- k_fold(sample, iris, 4)  # distribution of folds tbl <- NULL for (f in folds) {  tbl <- rbind(tbl, table(f$Species)) } head(tbl) #>      setosa versicolor virginica #> [1,]     10         14        13 #> [2,]     11         12        14 #> [3,]     13         11        14 #> [4,]     16         13         9

PCA — dt_pca
text: PCA (Principal Component Analysis) unsupervised dimensionality reduction technique used data analysis machine learning. transforms dataset possibly correlated variables new set uncorrelated variables called principal components.

title: PCA — dt_pca
text: attribute target attribute model building components number components PCA

title: PCA — dt_pca
code: mypca <- dt_pca("Species") # Automatically fitting number of components mypca <- fit(mypca, iris) iris.pca <- transform(mypca, iris) head(iris.pca) #>        PC1       PC2 Species #> 1 2.640270 -5.204041  setosa #> 2 2.670730 -4.666910  setosa #> 3 2.454606 -4.773636  setosa #> 4 2.545517 -4.648463  setosa #> 5 2.561228 -5.258629  setosa #> 6 2.975946 -5.707321  setosa head(mypca$pca.transf) #>                     PC1         PC2 #> Sepal.Length  0.5210659 -0.37741762 #> Sepal.Width  -0.2693474 -0.92329566 #> Petal.Length  0.5804131 -0.02449161 #> Petal.Width   0.5648565 -0.06694199 # Manual establishment of number of components mypca <- dt_pca("Species", 3) mypca <- fit(mypca, datasets::iris) iris.pca <- transform(mypca, iris) head(iris.pca) #>        PC1       PC2      PC3 Species #> 1 2.640270 -5.204041 2.488621  setosa #> 2 2.670730 -4.666910 2.466898  setosa #> 3 2.454606 -4.773636 2.288321  setosa #> 4 2.545517 -4.648463 2.212378  setosa #> 5 2.561228 -5.258629 2.392226  setosa #> 6 2.975946 -5.707321 2.437245  setosa head(mypca$pca.transf) #>                     PC1         PC2        PC3 #> Sepal.Length  0.5210659 -0.37741762  0.7195664 #> Sepal.Width  -0.2693474 -0.92329566 -0.2443818 #> Petal.Length  0.5804131 -0.02449161 -0.1421264 #> Petal.Width   0.5648565 -0.06694199 -0.6342727

title: evaluate — evaluate
text: evaluate learner performance. actual evaluate varies according type learner (clustering, classification, regression, time series regression)

title: evaluate — evaluate
code: data(iris) slevels <- levels(iris$Species) model <- cla_dtree("Species", slevels) model <- fit(model, iris) prediction <- predict(model, iris) predictand <- adjust_class_label(iris[,"Species"]) test_eval <- evaluate(model, predictand, prediction) test_eval$metrics #>    accuracy        f1 sensitivity specificity precision    recall #> 1 0.9822222 0.9733333   0.9733333   0.9866667 0.9733333 0.9733333

title: maximum curvature analysis — fit_curvature_max
text: Fitting curvature model sequence observations. extracts maximum curvature computed.

title: maximum curvature analysis — fit_curvature_max
text: Returns object class fit_curvature_max, inherits fit_curvature dal_transform classes. object contains list following elements: x: position maximum curvature reached. y: value maximum curvature occurs. yfit: value maximum curvature.

title: maximum curvature analysis — fit_curvature_max
code: x <- seq(from=1,to=10,by=0.5) dat <- data.frame(x = x, value = -log(x), variable = "log") myfit <- fit_curvature_max() res <- transform(myfit, dat$value) head(res) #>   x         y         yfit #> 1 9 -1.609438 9.224359e-08

title: minimum curvature analysis — fit_curvature_min
text: Fitting curvature model sequence observations. extracts minimum curvature computed.

title: minimum curvature analysis — fit_curvature_min
text: Returns object class fit_curvature_max, inherits fit_curvature dal_transform classes. object contains list following elements: x: position minimum curvature reached. y: value minimum curvature occurs. yfit: value minimum curvature.

title: minimum curvature analysis — fit_curvature_min
code: x <- seq(from=1,to=10,by=0.5) dat <- data.frame(x = x, value = log(x), variable = "log") myfit <- fit_curvature_min() res <- transform(myfit, dat$value) head(res) #>   x        y          yfit #> 1 9 1.609438 -9.224359e-08

title: k-fold sampling — k_fold
text: k-fold partition dataset using sampling method

title: k-fold sampling — k_fold
text: obj object data dataset k number folds

title: k-fold sampling — k_fold
code: #using random sampling sample <- sample_random()  # preparing dataset into four folds folds <- k_fold(sample, iris, 4)  # distribution of folds tbl <- NULL for (f in folds) {  tbl <- rbind(tbl, table(f$Species)) } head(tbl) #>      setosa versicolor virginica #> [1,]     12         13        12 #> [2,]     10         11        16 #> [3,]     16         11        11 #> [4,]     12         15        11

title: min-max normalization — minmax
code: data(iris) head(iris) #>   Sepal.Length Sepal.Width Petal.Length Petal.Width Species #> 1          5.1         3.5          1.4         0.2  setosa #> 2          4.9         3.0          1.4         0.2  setosa #> 3          4.7         3.2          1.3         0.2  setosa #> 4          4.6         3.1          1.5         0.2  setosa #> 5          5.0         3.6          1.4         0.2  setosa #> 6          5.4         3.9          1.7         0.4  setosa  trans <- minmax() trans <- fit(trans, iris) tiris <- transform(trans, iris) head(tiris) #>   Sepal.Length Sepal.Width Petal.Length Petal.Width Species #> 1   0.22222222   0.6250000   0.06779661  0.04166667  setosa #> 2   0.16666667   0.4166667   0.06779661  0.04166667  setosa #> 3   0.11111111   0.5000000   0.05084746  0.04166667  setosa #> 4   0.08333333   0.4583333   0.08474576  0.04166667  setosa #> 5   0.19444444   0.6666667   0.06779661  0.04166667  setosa #> 6   0.30555556   0.7916667   0.11864407  0.12500000  setosa  itiris <- inverse_transform(trans, tiris) head(itiris) #>   Sepal.Length Sepal.Width Petal.Length Petal.Width Species #> 1          5.1         3.5          1.4         0.2  setosa #> 2          4.9         3.0          1.4         0.2  setosa #> 3          4.7         3.2          1.3         0.2  setosa #> 4          4.6         3.1          1.5         0.2  setosa #> 5          5.0         3.6          1.4         0.2  setosa #> 6          5.4         3.9          1.7         0.4  setosa

title: Outliers — outliers
text: outliers class uses box-plot definition outliers. outlier value \(Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR\) higher \(Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR\). class remove outliers numeric attributes. Users can set alpha 3 remove extreme values.

title: Outliers — outliers
text: alpha boxplot outlier threshold (default 1.5, can 3.0 remove extreme values)

title: Outliers — outliers
code: # code for outlier removal out_obj <- outliers() # class for outlier analysis out_obj <- fit(out_obj, iris) # computing boundaries iris.clean <- transform(out_obj, iris) # returning cleaned dataset  #inspection of cleaned dataset nrow(iris.clean) #> [1] 146  idx <- attr(iris.clean, "idx") table(idx) #> idx #> FALSE  TRUE  #>   146     4  iris.outliers <- iris[idx,] iris.outliers #>    Sepal.Length Sepal.Width Petal.Length Petal.Width    Species #> 16          5.7         4.4          1.5         0.4     setosa #> 33          5.2         4.1          1.5         0.1     setosa #> 34          5.5         4.2          1.4         0.2     setosa #> 61          5.0         2.0          3.5         1.0 versicolor

title: Plot a time series chart — plot_ts_pred
text: x time index y time series yadj adjustment time series ypred prediction time series label_x x-axis title label_y y-axis title color color time series color_adjust color adjusted values color_prediction color predictions

title: Plot a time series chart — plot_ts_pred
Plot a time series chart — plot_ts_pred
code: data(sin_data) ts <- ts_data(sin_data$y, 0) ts_head(ts, 3) #>             t0 #> [1,] 0.0000000 #> [2,] 0.2474040 #> [3,] 0.4794255   samp <- ts_sample(ts, test_size= 5) io_train <- ts_projection(samp$train) io_test <- ts_projection(samp$test)  model <- ts_arima() model <- fit(model, x=io_train$input, y=io_train$output) adjust <- predict(model, io_train$input)  prediction <- predict(model, x=io_test$input, steps_ahead=5) prediction <- as.vector(prediction)  yvalues <- c(io_train$output, io_test$output) grf <- plot_ts_pred(y=yvalues, yadj=adjust, ypre=prediction) plot(grf)

title: DAL Predict — predictor
text: Ancestor class regression classification provides basis fit predict methods. Besides, action method proxies predict. example learner decision tree (cla_dtree)

title: DAL Predict — predictor
code: #See ?cla_dtree for a classification example using a decision tree

title: Decision Tree for regression — reg_dtree
text: Creates regression object uses Decision Tree method regression wraps tree library.

title: Decision Tree for regression — reg_dtree
code: data(Boston) model <- reg_dtree("medv")  # preparing dataset for random sampling sr <- sample_random() sr <- train_test(sr, Boston) train <- sr$train test <- sr$test  model <- fit(model, train)  test_prediction <- predict(model, test) test_predictand <- test[,"medv"] test_eval <- evaluate(model, test_predictand, test_prediction) test_eval$metrics #>        mse     smape #> 1 28.08633 0.1554412

title: knn regression — reg_knn
text: Creates regression object uses K-Nearest Neighbors (knn) method regression

title: knn regression — reg_knn
text: attribute attribute target model building k number k neighbors

title: knn regression — reg_knn
code: data(Boston) model <- reg_knn("medv", k=3)  # preparing dataset for random sampling sr <- sample_random() sr <- train_test(sr, Boston) train <- sr$train test <- sr$test  model <- fit(model, train)  test_prediction <- predict(model, test) test_predictand <- test[,"medv"] test_eval <- evaluate(model, test_predictand, test_prediction) test_eval$metrics #>        mse     smape #> 1 59.12315 0.2084534

title: MLP for regression — reg_mlp
text: Creates regression object uses Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) method. wraps nnet library.

title: MLP for regression — reg_mlp
text: attribute attribute target model building size number neurons hidden layers decay decay learning rate maxit number maximum iterations training

title: MLP for regression — reg_mlp
code: data(Boston) model <- reg_mlp("medv", size=5, decay=0.54)  # preparing dataset for random sampling sr <- sample_random() sr <- train_test(sr, Boston) train <- sr$train test <- sr$test  model <- fit(model, train)  test_prediction <- predict(model, test) test_predictand <- test[,"medv"] test_eval <- evaluate(model, test_predictand, test_prediction) test_eval$metrics #>        mse     smape #> 1 17.07979 0.1169867

title: Random Forest for regression — reg_rf
text: Creates regression object uses Random Forest method. wraps randomForest library.

title: Random Forest for regression — reg_rf
text: attribute attribute target model building nodesize node size ntree number trees mtry number attributes build tree

title: Random Forest for regression — reg_rf
code: data(Boston) model <- reg_rf("medv", ntree=10)  # preparing dataset for random sampling sr <- sample_random() sr <- train_test(sr, Boston) train <- sr$train test <- sr$test  model <- fit(model, train)  test_prediction <- predict(model, test) test_predictand <- test[,"medv"] test_eval <- evaluate(model, test_predictand, test_prediction) test_eval$metrics #>        mse      smape #> 1 8.534316 0.09645675

title: SVM for regression — reg_svm
text: Creates regression object uses Support Vector Machine (SVM) method regression wraps e1071 library.

title: SVM for regression — reg_svm
text: attribute attribute target model building epsilon parameter controls width margin around separating hyperplane cost parameter controls trade-wide margin correctly classifying training data points kernel type kernel function used SVM algorithm (linear, radial, polynomial, sigmoid)

title: SVM for regression — reg_svm
code: data(Boston) model <- reg_svm("medv", epsilon=0.2,cost=40.000)  # preparing dataset for random sampling sr <- sample_random() sr <- train_test(sr, Boston) train <- sr$train test <- sr$test  model <- fit(model, train)  test_prediction <- predict(model, test) test_predictand <- test[,"medv"] test_eval <- evaluate(model, test_predictand, test_prediction) test_eval$metrics #>       mse     smape #> 1 12.4347 0.1094984

title: Regression Tune — reg_tune
text: base_model base model tuning folds number folds cross-validation

title: Regression Tune — reg_tune
code: # preparing dataset for random sampling data(Boston) sr <- sample_random() sr <- train_test(sr, Boston) train <- sr$train test <- sr$test  # hyper parameter setup tune <- reg_tune(reg_svm("medv")) ranges <- list(epsilon=seq(0,1,0.25), cost=seq(25,100,25), kernel = c("radial"))  # hyper parameter optimization model <- fit(tune, train, ranges)  test_prediction <- predict(model, test) test_predictand <- test[,"medv"] test_eval <- evaluate(model, test_predictand, test_prediction) test_eval$metrics #>        mse      smape #> 1 7.329456 0.09764215

Stratified sampling statistical method used population divided non-overlapping subgroups strata, sample selected stratum represent entire population. stratified sampling, sample selected way representative entire population variability within stratum minimized.

title: Smoothing — smoothing
text: Smoothing statistical technique used reduce noise signal dataset removing high-frequency components. smoothing level associated number bins used. alternative methods establish smoothing: equal interval, equal frequency, clustering.

title: Smoothing — smoothing
code: data(iris) obj <- smoothing_inter(n = 2) obj <- fit(obj, iris$Sepal.Length) sl.bi <- transform(obj, iris$Sepal.Length) table(sl.bi) #> sl.bi #> 5.32842105263158 6.73272727272727  #>               95               55  obj$interval #> [1] 4.3 6.1 7.9  entro <- evaluate(obj, as.factor(names(sl.bi)), iris$Species) entro$entropy #> [1] 1.191734

title: Smoothing by cluster — smoothing_cluster
text: Uses clustering method perform data smoothing. input vector divided clusters using k-means algorithm. mean cluster calculated used smoothed value observations within cluster.

title: Smoothing by cluster — smoothing_cluster
code: data(iris) obj <- smoothing_cluster(n = 2) obj <- fit(obj, iris$Sepal.Length) sl.bi <- transform(obj, iris$Sepal.Length) table(sl.bi) #> sl.bi #> 5.22409638554217 6.61044776119403  #>               83               67  obj$interval #> [1] 4.300000 5.917272 7.900000  entro <- evaluate(obj, as.factor(names(sl.bi)), iris$Species) entro$entropy #> [1] 1.12088

text: 'smoothing_freq' function used smooth given time series data aggregating observations within fixed frequency.

title: Smoothing by Freq — smoothing_freq
code: data(iris) obj <- smoothing_freq(n = 2) obj <- fit(obj, iris$Sepal.Length) sl.bi <- transform(obj, iris$Sepal.Length) table(sl.bi) #> sl.bi #> 5.19875    6.58  #>      80      70  obj$interval #> [1] 4.3 5.8 7.9  entro <- evaluate(obj, as.factor(names(sl.bi)), iris$Species) entro$entropy #> [1] 1.097573

ARIMA — ts_arima
text: Creates time series prediction object uses AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA). wraps forecast library.

title: ARIMA — ts_arima
code: data(sin_data) ts <- ts_data(sin_data$y, 0) ts_head(ts, 3) #>             t0 #> [1,] 0.0000000 #> [2,] 0.2474040 #> [3,] 0.4794255  samp <- ts_sample(ts, test_size = 5) io_train <- ts_projection(samp$train) io_test <- ts_projection(samp$test)  model <- ts_arima() model <- fit(model, x=io_train$input, y=io_train$output)  prediction <- predict(model, x=io_test$input[1,], steps_ahead=5) prediction <- as.vector(prediction) output <- as.vector(io_test$output)  ev_test <- evaluate(model, output, prediction) ev_test #> $values #> [1]  0.41211849  0.17388949 -0.07515112 -0.31951919 -0.54402111 #>  #> $prediction #> [1] 0.6011374 0.5784414 0.5566023 0.5355877 0.5153665 #>  #> $smape #> [1] 1.489711 #>  #> $mse #> [1] 0.4904025 #>  #> $metrics #>         mse    smape #> 1 0.4904025 1.489711 #>

title: Augmentation by awareness — ts_aug_awareness
text: Time series data augmentation technique used increase size diversity time series dataset creating new instances original data transformations modifications. goal improve performance machine learning models trained time series data reducing overfitting improving generalization. Awareness reinforce recent data preferably.

title: Augmentation by awareness smooth — ts_aug_awaresmooth
text: Time series data augmentation technique used increase size diversity time series dataset creating new instances original data transformations modifications. goal improve performance machine learning models trained time series data reducing overfitting improving generalization. Awareness Smooth reinforce recent data preferably. also smooths noise data.

title: Augmentation by flip — ts_aug_flip
text: Time series data augmentation technique used increase size diversity time series dataset creating new instances original data transformations modifications. goal improve performance machine learning models trained time series data reducing overfitting improving generalization. Flip mirror sliding observations relative mean sliding windows.

title: Augmentation by jitter — ts_aug_jitter
text: Time series data augmentation technique used increase size diversity time series dataset creating new instances original data transformations modifications. goal improve performance machine learning models trained time series data reducing overfitting improving generalization. jitter adds random noise data point time series.

title: Augmentation by shrink — ts_aug_shrink
text: Time series data augmentation technique used increase size diversity time series dataset creating new instances original data transformations modifications. goal improve performance machine learning models trained time series data reducing overfitting improving generalization. stretch data augmentation decreasing volatility time series.

title: Augmentation by stretch — ts_aug_stretch
text: Time series data augmentation technique used increase size diversity time series dataset creating new instances original data transformations modifications. goal improve performance machine learning models trained time series data reducing overfitting improving generalization. stretch data augmentation increasing volatility time series.

title: Augmentation by wormhole — ts_aug_wormhole
text: Time series data augmentation technique used increase size diversity time series dataset creating new instances original data transformations modifications. goal improve performance machine learning models trained time series data reducing overfitting improving generalization. Wormhole data augmentation removing lagged terms adding old terms.

title: ts_data — ts_data
text: Time series data structure used DAL Toolbox. receives vector (representing time series) matrix y (representing sliding windows). Internal ts_data matrix sliding windows size sw. sw equals zero, store time series single matrix column.

title: Time Series Sample — ts_sample
text: Separates ts_data training test. separates test size last observations minus offset. offset important allow replication different recent origins. data test uses number rows ts_data minus test size offset.

title: Time Series Sample — ts_sample
text: ts time series data. test_size integer: size test data (default = 1). offset integer: starting point (default = 0).

title: SVM — ts_svm
text: Creates time series prediction object uses Support Vector Machine (SVM). wraps e1071 library.

title: SVM — ts_svm
code: ts_svm(   preprocess = NA,   input_size = NA,   kernel = "radial",   epsilon = 0,   cost = 10 )

title: SVM — ts_svm
text: preprocess normalization input_size input size machine learning model kernel SVM kernel (linear, radial, polynomial, sigmoid) epsilon error threshold cost cost

title: Time Series Tune — ts_tune
text: input_size input size machine learning model base_model base model tuning folds number folds cross-validation

title: Time Series Tune — ts_tune
code: data(sin_data) ts <- ts_data(sin_data$y, 10) ts_head(ts, 3) #>             t9        t8        t7        t6        t5        t4        t3 #> [1,] 0.0000000 0.2474040 0.4794255 0.6816388 0.8414710 0.9489846 0.9974950 #> [2,] 0.2474040 0.4794255 0.6816388 0.8414710 0.9489846 0.9974950 0.9839859 #> [3,] 0.4794255 0.6816388 0.8414710 0.9489846 0.9974950 0.9839859 0.9092974 #>             t2        t1        t0 #> [1,] 0.9839859 0.9092974 0.7780732 #> [2,] 0.9092974 0.7780732 0.5984721 #> [3,] 0.7780732 0.5984721 0.3816610  samp <- ts_sample(ts, test_size = 5) io_train <- ts_projection(samp$train) io_test <- ts_projection(samp$test)  tune <- ts_tune(input_size=c(3:5), base_model = ts_elm(ts_norm_gminmax())) ranges <- list(nhid = 1:5, actfun=c('sig', 'radbas', 'tribas', 'relu', 'purelin'))  # Generic model tunning model <- fit(tune, x=io_train$input, y=io_train$output, ranges)  prediction <- predict(model, x=io_test$input[1,], steps_ahead=5) prediction <- as.vector(prediction) output <- as.vector(io_test$output)  ev_test <- evaluate(model, output, prediction) ev_test #> $values #> [1]  0.41211849  0.17388949 -0.07515112 -0.31951919 -0.54402111 #>  #> $prediction #> [1]  0.41211849  0.17388949 -0.07515112 -0.31951919 -0.54402111 #>  #> $smape #> [1] 4.261128e-13 #>  #> $mse #> [1] 1.666806e-26 #>  #> $metrics #>            mse        smape #> 1 1.666806e-26 4.261128e-13 #>

title: z-score normalization — zscore
text: Scale data using z-score normalization. \(zscore = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\), \(\mu\) mean \(x\) \(\sigma\) standard deviation.

title: z-score normalization — zscore
text: nmean new mean normalized data nsd new standard deviation normalized data

title: z-score normalization — zscore
code: data(iris) head(iris) #>   Sepal.Length Sepal.Width Petal.Length Petal.Width Species #> 1          5.1         3.5          1.4         0.2  setosa #> 2          4.9         3.0          1.4         0.2  setosa #> 3          4.7         3.2          1.3         0.2  setosa #> 4          4.6         3.1          1.5         0.2  setosa #> 5          5.0         3.6          1.4         0.2  setosa #> 6          5.4         3.9          1.7         0.4  setosa  trans <- zscore() trans <- fit(trans, iris) tiris <- transform(trans, iris) head(tiris) #>   Sepal.Length Sepal.Width Petal.Length Petal.Width Species #> 1   -0.8976739  1.01560199    -1.335752   -1.311052  setosa #> 2   -1.1392005 -0.13153881    -1.335752   -1.311052  setosa #> 3   -1.3807271  0.32731751    -1.392399   -1.311052  setosa #> 4   -1.5014904  0.09788935    -1.279104   -1.311052  setosa #> 5   -1.0184372  1.24503015    -1.335752   -1.311052  setosa #> 6   -0.5353840  1.93331463    -1.165809   -1.048667  setosa  itiris <- inverse_transform(trans, tiris) head(itiris) #>   Sepal.Length Sepal.Width Petal.Length Petal.Width Species #> 1          5.1         3.5          1.4         0.2  setosa #> 2          4.9         3.0          1.4         0.2  setosa #> 3          4.7         3.2          1.3         0.2  setosa #> 4          4.6         3.1          1.5         0.2  setosa #> 5          5.0         3.6          1.4         0.2  setosa #> 6          5.4         3.9          1.7         0.4  setosa
